stem: A previously well 22-month-old presents to her GP with 36 hours of fever to 39 degrees, persistent night-time crying, and tugging at her right ear. She has had 2 prior episodes of otitis media in the last 12 months. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Her parents have tried paracetamol with limited relief.

data_table.Examination: Right tympanic membrane bulging, erythematous and dull; left tympanic membrane normal; no mastoid tenderness; no neck stiffness

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure parents and arrange GP review in 48 hours with adequate analgesia
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is reasonable in mild disease over 2 years but fever over 39 with bulging membrane in a child under 2 with prior episodes warrants antibiotics. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Commence oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days plus analgesia
options.rationale: Under 2 years with bulging membrane, fever and prior recurrent episodes meets AU criteria for immediate antibiotics; amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days is first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic 2023)

options.text: Prescribe oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for 10 days from today
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for treatment failure after 48 to 72 hours of amoxicillin, complicated otitis media, or recurrent disease in high-risk populations. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer same-day to ENT for tympanocentesis and culture-directed antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Tympanocentesis is reserved for treatment failure, immunocompromised hosts, or suspected suppurative complications, not first presentation. (Source: RCH CPG: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Order pure tone audiometry and refer to ENT for grommet insertion in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Audiometry and grommets are for persistent middle ear effusion or recurrent disease (more than 3 episodes in 6 months) after acute treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Otitis media with effusion)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Otitis media with effusion

explanation.summary: AU treatment of acute otitis media in non-Indigenous children is risk-stratified. Watchful waiting is preferred for mild disease over 2 years with shared decision-making and analgesia. Antibiotics are indicated for children under 2 years with bilateral disease or bulging tympanic membranes, systemic features (temperature >= 39, vomiting, lethargy), perforation with discharge, or in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children regardless of severity. Amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days is first-line. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for treatment failure or complicated disease.
key_points: Under 2 with bulging membrane + fever = antibiotics (not watchful waiting).
Amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days first-line.
Amoxicillin-clavulanate for failure at 48 to 72 hours or complicated disease.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children: antibiotics regardless of severity.
Persistent effusion at 3 months or recurrent (over 3 in 6 months) - audiology + ENT.
explanation.pearls: Topical analgesia (lignocaine ear drops 2 percent) gives short-term relief but is contraindicated with perforated tympanic membrane - always inspect first.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Otitis media with effusion

stem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the GP within 10 minutes of eating a peanut butter biscuit at a friend's birthday party. She has known peanut allergy diagnosed at 18 months by RAST and has a current anaphylaxis action plan. Her mother saw her develop facial swelling and difficulty breathing in the car. She has had no previous anaphylactic episodes.

data_table.Examination: Generalised urticarial rash, swollen lips and tongue, audible inspiratory stridor, expiratory wheeze bilaterally, alert but distressed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg into the mid lateral thigh now
options.rationale: AU first-line for anaphylaxis in children 10 to 20 kg is intramuscular adrenaline 0.15 mg (150 mcg) into the mid lateral thigh; repeat every 5 minutes if needed. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines 2023; eTG)

options.text: Give oral promethazine 0.5 mg/kg and observe in the waiting room area
options.rationale: Oral antihistamines have no role in acute anaphylaxis and delay definitive treatment; IM adrenaline is first-line. (Source: ASCIA anaphylaxis guidelines 2023)

options.text: Commence intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and arrange transfer by ambulance
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not treat acute anaphylaxis; recent evidence shows no impact on biphasic reactions. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

options.text: Nebulise salbutamol 2.5 mg and oxygen 6 L/min via face mask before any other agent
options.rationale: Bronchodilators address asthma component but do not reverse upper airway oedema, hypotension or biphasic reactions; adrenaline first. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

options.text: Administer subcutaneous adrenaline 0.15 mg and call for paediatric emergency support
options.rationale: Subcutaneous adrenaline absorption is slower and unreliable in shock; the intramuscular route into the lateral thigh achieves peak plasma levels at 8 to 10 minutes. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is a severe systemic IgE-mediated reaction with airway, breathing or circulation involvement. AU first-line treatment is intramuscular adrenaline into the mid lateral thigh: 0.15 mg for 10 to 20 kg, 0.3 mg for over 20 kg, 0.5 mg for adults. The dose is repeated every 5 minutes as needed. Supportive care includes high flow oxygen, IV fluids for hypotension, and bronchodilators for bronchospasm. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts only and have no role in acute reversal.
key_points: IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg up to 0.5 mg into the mid lateral thigh - first-line.
Repeat every 5 minutes if no response.
Lay patient flat (not sitting up) due to risk of empty ventricle syndrome.
Add IV crystalloid 20 mL/kg bolus for hypotension; nebulised salbutamol for bronchospasm.
Observe minimum 4 hours after symptom resolution; longer if biphasic risk factors.
explanation.pearls: Empty ventricle syndrome - sitting an anaphylaxis patient upright when hypotensive can precipitate cardiac arrest from inadequate venous return; always lay flat with legs elevated unless airway compromise prevents.

subtopic_detail: Cow's milk protein allergy in infant
stem: A 10-week-old formula-fed infant is brought to the GP with 4 weeks of increasing vomiting after feeds, loose stools with mucus, and intermittent eczema. He has dropped from the 50th to the 15th centile for weight. Both parents have atopy. Past treatment with thickened formula and antacid trial has not improved symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Mild facial eczema, fussy but consolable, abdomen soft and non-distended, hydration adequate, no abdominal mass
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, allergy testing not yet performed

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial soy-based infant formula for 2 weeks and review symptoms thereafter
options.rationale: Soy formula has up to 30 percent cross-reactivity with cow's milk protein under 6 months; not first-line for suspected non-IgE CMPA. (Source: ASCIA CMPA 2022)

options.text: Continue thickened cow's milk formula and add proton pump inhibitor twice daily
options.rationale: PPI is not recommended for infant reflux without confirmed acid-related disease and does not address allergy-driven gastrointestinal symptoms. (Source: NASPGHAN-ESPGHAN 2018)

options.text: Commence extensively hydrolysed formula for 2 to 4 weeks as a diagnostic trial
options.rationale: Extensively hydrolysed formula (eHF) is first-line for suspected non-IgE CMPA; symptom improvement within 2 to 4 weeks supports the diagnosis and warrants formal challenge later. (Source: ASCIA CMPA 2022)

options.text: Order serum specific IgE for cow's milk protein and refer to immunology urgently
options.rationale: Specific IgE confirms IgE-mediated allergy but the picture here suggests non-IgE; dietary elimination and challenge is the gold standard. (Source: ASCIA CMPA 2022)

options.text: Arrange upper gastrointestinal endoscopy and biopsy as a same-week outpatient procedure
options.rationale: Endoscopy is for failure of dietary trial, suspected eosinophilic oesophagitis or alarm features (bleeding, dysphagia); not first-line. (Source: NASPGHAN-ESPGHAN 2018)

explanation.summary: Non-IgE mediated cow's milk protein allergy presents with vomiting, loose mucousy stools, eczema, poor feeding and faltering growth in formula-fed infants. AU first-line management is a 2 to 4 week trial of extensively hydrolysed formula (eHF); amino acid formula is reserved for severe symptoms or eHF failure. Symptom improvement supports the diagnosis and is followed by a structured rechallenge at 6 to 12 months. Soy formula is not recommended under 6 months due to cross-reactivity and phytoestrogen concerns.
key_points: Symptoms: vomiting, loose mucousy stools, eczema, faltering growth, infant colic.
First-line: eHF trial 2 to 4 weeks.
Amino acid formula if severe (anaphylaxis history, eosinophilic disease, multiple food allergies).
Avoid soy under 6 months; cross-reactivity up to 30 percent.
Reintroduce milk at 12 to 24 months via supervised home challenge or milk ladder.
explanation.pearls: In breastfed infants with CMPA, maternal strict cow's milk-free diet (including all dairy and hidden sources) for 2 to 4 weeks is first-line; calcium and vitamin D supplementation must be considered for mother.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Cow's milk protein allergy

subtopic_detail: Incomplete Kawasaki disease in young infant
stem: A 4-month-old boy is admitted on day 7 of fever to 39.5 degrees that has not responded to broad-spectrum IV antibiotics started 48 hours ago. Multiple cultures (blood, urine, CSF) are negative. He has had 5 days of rash, bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis since day 3, and ongoing irritability. Lips appear cracked on review today. He has no extremity changes and no cervical lymphadenopathy.

data_table.Examination: Diffuse macular rash, bilateral bulbar conjunctival injection without exudate, dry cracked lips with strawberry tongue developing, no peripheral oedema, no lymphadenopathy >= 1.5 cm
data_table.Investigations: CRP 142 mg/L, ESR 88 mm/h, WCC 18.5 x10^9/L, Plt 612 x10^9/L, ALT 110 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, urine WCC 35 x10^6/L sterile, echocardiogram: small RCA dilation z-score 2.3

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue broad-spectrum antibiotics for a further 5 days and re-image with echocardiogram weekly
options.rationale: With 7 days of fever, classic mucocutaneous features developing, elevated inflammatory markers and a coronary artery z-score above 2, treatment for Kawasaki must not be delayed. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease)

options.text: Administer IVIG 2 g/kg over 12 hours plus high-dose oral aspirin 30 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months with prolonged unexplained fever, supportive labs (CRP >= 30, ESR >= 40, platelets, albumin, ALT, sterile pyuria) and echocardiographic changes meet AHA/RCH criteria for incomplete Kawasaki; treat with IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin without delay. (Source: AHA scientific statement on Kawasaki disease 2017; RCH CPG)
source_refs: AHA scientific statement on Kawasaki disease 2017

options.text: Initiate intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg pulse therapy as monotherapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are adjuncts (Kobayashi score high-risk or IVIG failure), not first-line monotherapy; IVIG remains the primary disease-modifying treatment. (Source: AHA 2017)
source_refs: AHA scientific statement on Kawasaki disease 2017

options.text: Commence infliximab 5 mg/kg infusion before any IVIG or corticosteroid therapy
options.rationale: Infliximab is reserved for IVIG-refractory Kawasaki disease (persistent fever 36 hours after IVIG) not first-line. (Source: AHA 2017)
source_refs: AHA scientific statement on Kawasaki disease 2017

options.text: Refer for cardiac MRI before any treatment decision is finalised by paediatric cardiology
options.rationale: Echocardiography is the imaging modality of choice in acute Kawasaki; MRI is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty in subacute phase and treatment must not be delayed. (Source: AHA 2017)
source_refs: AHA scientific statement on Kawasaki disease 2017

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is more common under 6 months and over 5 years and accounts for most coronary artery complications because diagnosis is delayed. AHA criteria allow treatment when fever lasts at least 5 days, 2 or 3 classic criteria are met, CRP at or above 30 mg/L or ESR at or above 40 mm/h, plus 3 or more supportive labs (anaemia, platelets above 450, albumin under 30, ALT raised, WCC above 15, sterile pyuria) OR echocardiogram changes (z-score above 2, perivascular brightness, lack of tapering). Treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion plus aspirin 30 to 50 mg/kg daily until afebrile 48 to 72 hours then 3 to 5 mg/kg daily for 6 to 8 weeks.
key_points: Treat at day 5 of fever even with incomplete features if labs and echo support diagnosis.
IVIG 2 g/kg single dose + high-dose aspirin (30 to 50 mg/kg) acutely.
Adjunctive steroids for high Kobayashi score (Japanese cohort) or AU-style high risk.
Echocardiogram at diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 to 8 weeks to monitor coronaries.
Coronary artery z-score above 2 needs cardiology follow-up; above 10 = giant aneurysm.
explanation.pearls: Children with Kawasaki disease should not receive live vaccines (MMR, varicella) for at least 11 months after IVIG due to passive antibody interference with vaccine response.

sources.label: AHA scientific statement on Kawasaki disease 2017

stem: An 8-year-old boy with epilepsy on sodium valproate has had a convulsive seizure that began 35 minutes ago. He has received 2 doses of intranasal midazolam (10 mg total over 10 minutes) and a single IV levetiracetam loading dose (60 mg/kg over 15 minutes, finished 5 minutes ago). The seizure continues with ongoing rhythmic limb jerking. IV access is patent. No metabolic or structural triggers identified.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 162, RR 34, SpO2 92% on 6 L/min via mask, BP 102/64, T 37.8°C, BSL 7.2 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Ongoing generalised tonic-clonic movements, jaw clenched, pupils 4 mm equal, no focal deficits between jerks, airway protected with adjunct, no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: BSL 7.2, capillary VBG pH 7.28 lactate 4.2; recent valproate level 360 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg loading dose with concurrent fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Two adequate doses of two different agents have failed; this defines refractory status and requires escalation to a third agent or induction. (Source: PREDICT status epilepticus guideline 2022)

options.text: Commence IV propofol infusion at 4 mg/kg/h immediately without prior tracheal intubation
options.rationale: Propofol infusion at this rate without RSI risks airway compromise and propofol-related infusion syndrome; if induction is chosen, intubation precedes infusion. (Source: PREDICT 2022)

options.text: Administer IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Refractory status (failure of 2 agents) requires a third-line antiepileptic or RSI; phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring is appropriate next step before induction (although intubation/midazolam infusion is alternative). (Source: PREDICT 2022; RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Give intramuscular paraldehyde 0.3 mL/kg into the right lateral thigh once now
options.rationale: Paraldehyde is rarely available now and is not part of current AU paediatric algorithm. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Order urgent EEG and withhold further therapy until non-convulsive status is excluded
options.rationale: Convulsive status is clinical; withholding treatment risks brain injury and ongoing seizure - EEG can be obtained during ongoing therapy. (Source: PREDICT 2022)

explanation.summary: Paediatric refractory status epilepticus is defined as ongoing seizure after 2 adequate doses of 2 antiepileptic agents (typically benzodiazepine then second-line: levetiracetam, phenytoin, or sodium valproate). PREDICT and RCH algorithms recommend a third-line antiepileptic (phenytoin if not yet used, or sodium valproate) with concurrent preparation for rapid sequence induction. Failure of third-line escalates to RSI with thiopentone or midazolam infusion in PICU. Cardiac monitoring during phenytoin is essential due to hypotension and arrhythmia risk.
key_points: Refractory status = failure of 2 agents at 30 minutes.
Third-line: phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 min with ECG monitoring; alternative valproate 40 mg/kg.
Escalation to RSI with thiopentone or midazolam infusion if 3rd agent fails.
Continuous EEG monitoring in PICU for refractory and super-refractory status.
Reverse triggers (sodium, glucose, calcium, magnesium, drug levels, infection).
explanation.pearls: Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy presents under 12 months with intractable seizures; trial IV pyridoxine 100 mg in refractory status under 18 months even without family history.
